is the perturbation mode after writing the density as the incoherent state plus a small correction,
is the growth or decay rate of the perturbation mode.
Re means take the real part. The primed symbols theta prime and omega prime are dummy integration variables, and g of omega prime is the natural-frequency distribution evaluated at omega prime.
Equation 17 says that a small perturbation

is shaped by two effects at once: advection in theta from each oscillator's own natural frequency omega, and mean-field feedback from the population average encoded by the double integral. The right-hand side lambda times mu means we are looking for perturbation modes that grow or decay exponentially in time.

This equation comes from writing the density as the incoherent state plus a small perturbation mu tilde, then setting mu tilde equals e to the lambda t times mu and ignoring terms nonlinear in mu. The accompanying normalization condition appears as Eq. 18.

blocks.value: Conceptually, Eq. 17 turns the synchronization question into an eigenvalue problem in the section Synchronization in the limit N equals infinity. You use it specifically when asking whether the incoherent state is linearly stable in the noiseless infinite-N limit. If some admissible lambda has positive real part, incoherence is unstable; if all admissible lambdas have negative real part, incoherence is linearly stable. This is a different question from the earlier self-consistency equations, which characterize synchronized branches once they exist.
Open a visual showing how a tiny perturbation can either decay or grow under Eq. 17.
